Paul closes his letter to Titus with a sobering reminder: you were once a fool. Not everyone else—you. Before God's kindness appeared in Christ, Paul writes, we were disobedient, enslaved to desires, living in malice and envy, hating and being hated. The starkness of this self-indictment is meant to break something in us. It is not written for those who think themselves righteous, but for those willing to see themselves as they truly were before grace intervened. This is the foundation for everything that follows: genuine humility about our former condition produces genuine gentleness toward others. When you remember that you were once foolish, you stop treating the foolish with contempt. When you remember that you were enslaved to desires, you stop viewing the enslaved to desire as beyond reach. Paul is saying that Christian conduct flows not from moral superiority but from rescued gratitude.

What Paul names as "the kindness of God our Savior and His love for mankind" is nothing less than the Incarnation itself—God appearing in mercy when we deserved judgment. And His salvation comes not through anything we accomplished, but through His mercy alone, enacted through the washing of new birth and the abundant pouring out of the Holy Spirit through Jesus Christ. This is not self-improvement dressed in religious language. This is death and resurrection. This is the replacement of what we were with something entirely new. We are justified by grace, not by our works, and this justification makes us heirs of eternal life. Paul wants Titus to emphasize this truth so forcefully that it transforms how believers live—not to earn their salvation, which is already complete, but because those who have truly grasped they are saved by grace alone cannot help but devote themselves to good works.

Yet Paul also cuts straight to a danger that threatens the church: divisions created by people who love arguments more than truth, who quarrel about genealogies and the law for the sake of quarreling. These controversies are pointless and worthless. A divisive person—someone who creates factions, who refuses correction—must be rejected after two warnings. This is not cruelty; it is clarity. Such a man is self-condemned; his own choices have made him what he is. The church cannot afford to let one person's need to be right over others destroy the unity purchased by Christ's blood. Paul's instruction here is uncompromising: protect the gospel community from those who use doctrine as a weapon rather than receive it as food.

The letter ends not with grand theology but with the ordinary work of the gospel. Tychicus is coming. Titus is to go meet Paul. Zenas and Apollos need provision. The church must learn to meet pressing needs so it will not be unfruitful. This is the shape of Christian life: remembering that we were saved entirely by grace, conducting ourselves with gentleness toward all, protecting the unity of the church from divisive voices, and then simply doing the work in front of us—meeting needs, providing hospitality, caring for those whom God has placed in our path. The gospel begins in grace and ends in the wearing ordinary faithfulness of people who refuse to forget what they were and what they have been given.
